title: 01_load_sales_export.ipynb
descriptionMd:
## Mission: Load the Monthly Sales Export

Your teammate exported June's sales from a European billing tool and asked you to total the revenue. The plan is simple: load the file with pandas, check the columns, and sum the `amount` column.

The notebook crashes on the very last line with a `KeyError` saying `amount` doesn't exist — even though you can open the file and clearly see an amount column. Look at what pandas actually loaded (the printed columns are a big hint) and fix the way the file is read so the three columns come through separately.
initialCode:
import pandas as pd

# Load the monthly sales export and inspect it
sales = pd.read_csv("sales_june.csv")

print("columns:", list(sales.columns))
print("rows:", len(sales))

# Total the revenue for the month
total = sales["amount"].sum()
print(f"total revenue: {total}")
traceback:
columns: ['date;product;amount']
rows: 30
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
KeyError                                  Traceback (most recent call last)
Cell In[1], line 11
      9 # Total the revenue for the month
---> 11 total = sales["amount"].sum()

KeyError: 'amount'

options.label: Tell read_csv the file is semicolon-separated with sep=";"
options.patchCode:
import pandas as pd

# Load the monthly sales export and inspect it
sales = pd.read_csv("sales_june.csv", sep=";")

print("columns:", list(sales.columns))
print("rows:", len(sales))

# Total the revenue for the month
total = sales["amount"].sum()
print(f"total revenue: {total}")
options.rationale: `read_csv` assumes commas separate the values, but this export uses semicolons (common in regions where the comma is the decimal mark). With the wrong separator pandas treats each whole line as one big value, so you get a single column literally named `date;product;amount`. Passing `sep=";"` tells pandas where the real column boundaries are, and all three columns load correctly.

options.label: Pass header=None so pandas stops treating row one as headers
options.patchCode:
import pandas as pd

# Load the monthly sales export and inspect it
sales = pd.read_csv("sales_june.csv", header=None)

print("columns:", list(sales.columns))
print("rows:", len(sales))

# Total the revenue for the month
total = sales["amount"].sum()
print(f"total revenue: {total}")
options.resultLog:
columns: [0]
rows: 31
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
KeyError                                  Traceback (most recent call last)
Cell In[1], line 11
---> 11 total = sales["amount"].sum()

KeyError: 'amount'
options.rationale: `header=None` only changes whether the first row is read as column names or as data — it does nothing about the separator. pandas still splits on commas, so you still get one column (now numbered `0` and with the header row counted as data). The problem was never the header; it was the delimiter.

options.label: Load it as-is, then split the single column on ";" afterwards
options.patchCode:
import pandas as pd

# Load the monthly sales export and inspect it
sales = pd.read_csv("sales_june.csv")
sales = sales["date;product;amount"].str.split(";", expand=True)

print("columns:", list(sales.columns))
print("rows:", len(sales))

# Total the revenue for the month
total = sales["amount"].sum()
print(f"total revenue: {total}")
options.resultLog:
columns: [0, 1, 2]
rows: 30
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
KeyError                                  Traceback (most recent call last)
Cell In[1], line 12
---> 12 total = sales["amount"].sum()

KeyError: 'amount'
options.rationale: Splitting the merged column by hand does separate the values, but `expand=True` hands back plain numbered columns `0, 1, 2` with no names — so `sales["amount"]` still fails, and the amounts are now text. It is fragile busywork. Letting `read_csv` parse the file with the right `sep` keeps the real headers and the numeric types for free.

hints: Read the first printed line carefully: pandas loaded a single column whose name is `date;product;amount`. If the three names are stuck together, what character is actually sitting between them — and what character did pandas expect?
The fix is an argument to `pd.read_csv` on the line that loads the file, not anything you do afterwards. Look up the `sep` (separator) parameter.
explanationMd:
### Why the bug occurs
A CSV file is just text, with some character separating the values on each line. `pd.read_csv` defaults to a comma, but this file separates values with semicolons. When the expected separator never appears, pandas finds no column boundaries and loads the entire line as one value — so the DataFrame has a single column whose name is the whole header line, `date;product;amount`. Asking for `sales["amount"]` then raises `KeyError` because no column by that name exists.

### Why the fix is correct
`sep=";"` tells `read_csv` which character marks the end of one value and the start of the next. With the correct separator, pandas splits each line into three fields, reads the first row as the column names, and infers numeric types for the amounts — so `sales["amount"].sum()` works. Fixing the read is better than patching the data afterwards because you keep the real column names and correct dtypes from the start.
recruiterReview: Nice, calm debugging — you read what pandas *actually* loaded instead of assuming. The merged column name was the tell, and fixing it at the `read_csv` call (rather than splitting strings by hand) is exactly the right instinct: load the data correctly once and everything downstream is clean. Worth remembering that European exports often pair a `;` separator with a `,` decimal mark, so `decimal=","` sometimes rides along with `sep=";"`. Approved. ✅

tutorial.bodyMd:
## Reading a CSV the Way It Was Actually Saved

Welcome — this is one of the very first things every data analyst learns, and it trips up almost everyone once.

### What is a CSV?
A **CSV** ("comma-separated values") file is a plain-text table. Each line is one row, and a chosen character — the **delimiter** (or **separator**) — marks where one cell ends and the next begins. Despite the name, that character is not always a comma. Files exported from European software very often use a **semicolon** (`;`), because in many countries the comma is used as the decimal point (so `1,5` means one-and-a-half).

### What is a DataFrame?
When pandas reads a CSV it builds a **DataFrame**: a table in memory with named **columns** (like `amount`) and numbered **rows**. You grab a column by name with square brackets, e.g. `df["amount"]`.

### The mechanism of this bug
`pd.read_csv` has to guess nothing about the delimiter — by default it assumes a comma. If the file actually uses semicolons, pandas scans each line, never finds a comma, and concludes the line is a single value. So your three columns collapse into **one** column whose name is the entire header line: `date;product;amount`. Any attempt to read a real column name fails.

### A tiny worked example
Suppose `mini.csv` contains:
```
name;score
Ada;90
Lin;85
```
Reading it the default way gives one column:
```python
pd.read_csv("mini.csv").columns
# Index(['name;score'], dtype='object')
```
Telling pandas the real separator fixes it:
```python
pd.read_csv("mini.csv", sep=";").columns
# Index(['name', 'score'], dtype='object')
```
Now `df["score"].sum()` returns `175`.

### How to spot it
Whenever a `KeyError` claims a column is missing that you can plainly see in the file, **print `df.columns` first**. If several headers are mashed together into one name, you loaded with the wrong delimiter.

### Common beginner mistakes
- Assuming every `.csv` uses commas — always glance at the raw first line.
- Reaching for `header=None` when the columns look wrong; that controls headers, not the delimiter.
- Splitting the merged column by hand afterwards instead of fixing the `read_csv` call.
- Forgetting that semicolon files often also need `decimal=","` so numbers parse as numbers.

You'll hit this bug the first time you open a file someone exported from Excel in another country.

videos.title: pandas read_csv delimiter / separator explained
videos.searchQuery: pandas read_csv sep delimiter semicolon csv tutorial

videos.title: Inspecting a DataFrame with columns, head, and shape
videos.searchQuery: pandas dataframe head columns shape inspect beginner

conceptCard:
This mission is about **loading data correctly**. Two ideas to know:

- A **CSV** is a text table, and a **delimiter** (a comma, a semicolon, …) separates the values on each line.
- pandas reads a CSV into a **DataFrame** — a table with named columns you pick out like `df["amount"]`.

The code loads a sales file and tries to total a column, but the file isn't comma-separated. Find the problem and fix the load.

lineNotes.noteMd: Loads the **pandas** library and nicknames it `pd` — the standard toolkit for working with tables of data.
`pd.read_csv(...)` reads a CSV file into a **DataFrame** (a table). By default it assumes **commas** separate the values — which is exactly the bug here.
`sales.columns` is the list of column names. Printing it is how you check the file loaded into the columns you expect.
`sales["amount"]` picks out the amount column, and `.sum()` adds all its values together.

takeaway: read_csv assumes commas — when columns come through merged, tell it the real delimiter with sep=.
